I think LeBron's greatness has come in three distinct parts. First, his extraordinary athleticism, which in his prime almost no one could match. Later, his understanding of the game, with a mastery of in-game tactics and series-wide strategy that again very few have equaled. And last, his incredible longevity to still play at an all-star level into his 40s, which no one has ever done before. Any one of these three would have made for a great career, and any two would lock up a Hall of Fame selection.

A big part of it is how coordinated and controlled he was. Lebron basically has perfect jumping and landing mechanics. Watch how he lands on all of these chase down blocks and dunks in traffic. His feet are almost always shoulder width apart and his knees are slightly bent. If he does land on one leg, it's never at a weird angle or with a straight leg. He also basically never lands on a foot. Compare that to someone like Rose or Ja Morant, and it's easy to see why they weren't durable and Lebron was.

The NFL doesn’t need to as much though because they are a behemoth and people love the game of football so much, they’ll watch even if their team isn’t playing. They benefit from scarcity. They set viewership records year after year basically regardless of who is good and who isn’t. The NFL will break viewership records for the next superbowl, even if Green Bay and Buffalo are the matchup. Market size doesn’t really affect them, nor do they live and die by super stars. Additionally, the NFL meta constantly changes. The game is not “solved” like basketball is where there is one clear winning play style (3s, FTs, layups). And if it were, the NFL isn’t afraid to change the rules (kickoffs, extra points). It’s very clear that the other big sports leagues still care about the sport itself, while Adam Silver views the actual sport of basketball as secondary to being an “entertainment product” to be consumed. I think this will genuinely hurt the NBA in the long run, even if short term gains might be good. I believe they’re basically mortgaging the future to try to build interest/fan engagement now.

As a Cavs fan who loves LeBron, I still think the "Finals to last place" narrative is one that wasn't totally fair. When LeBron left after "The Decision", the team didn't just lose LeBron, their entire strategy for how to build the roster was decimated and they decided to just nuke the team from orbit and tank hard (which led to getting Kyrie, and ironically, getting LeBron back). I forget the exact number, but something like 11 of the players who were rostered at the end of 2010 were not on the roster 1 year later. The team would have been bad without LeBron anyway, but probably not worst in the league bad if they had kept everyone else and tried to retool on the fly to max their wins instead of tanking to max their ping pong balls.

Idk, you have to wonder how much what we know about Wilt was true. His "1:58 800m" is widely questioned and there's no verification that it ever happened. "Most historians believe the 1:58.3 figure, along with his 49.0-second 440-yard dash, came from self-reported numbers or informal, unmapped practice times relayed by Wilt or his early coaches to reporters. Because Wilt was an extraordinary physical specimen, reporters of the 1950s and 60s frequently printed his training anecdotes as official athletic gospel without verifying box scores." Beyond basketball, Wilt is most well known for bragging and exaggerating, so when something is so crazy that it's hard to believe and it's coming from a renowned liar...it's probably not true. No doubt he was an unbelievable athlete, but if we want to talk about it, we need to only include things that we know are true.
